---
title: "De novo peptide sequencing from scratch"
subtitle: "Building the problem up from fragment ions, before any neural network shows up"
description: >
  What a mass spectrometer actually measures, why reading a peptide off a
  spectrum is hard, and how to build intuition for it with ~30 lines of Python, 
  before reaching for a transformer.
date: 2026-06-26
categories: [proteomics, de novo, deep learning, tutorial]
image: theoretical-spectrum.png   # generated by the first code cell; or drop in your own
image-alt: "Theoretical b/y fragment-ion spectrum"
draft: true   # <- remove this line to publish
---

> **Outline / draft.** Skeleton with a working first-principles code cell.
> Replace the prompts in blockquotes with your own writing.

## Why I'm writing this

> Hook: most explanations of de novo sequencing start at the model. I want to
> start one layer below, at the physics the model is approximating, because
> that's where the intuition (and most of the failure modes) actually live.

## What the instrument measures

> 2–3 paragraphs: precursor selection → fragmentation (HCD/CID) → the MS2
> spectrum as a sparse list of (m/z, intensity) peaks. Emphasise that the
> spectrum is a *noisy, incomplete* observation of the backbone cleavage events.

## Fragment ions from first principles

A peptide is a chain of residues. Fragment it along the backbone and you get two
complementary ion series: **b-ions** (N-terminal fragments) and **y-ions**
(C-terminal fragments). Everything else, the model, the scoring, the search, 
is built on top of this one fact. Here it is with nothing but residue masses:

::: {#cell-fig-theoretical-spectrum .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the from-scratch fragment-ion code"}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Monoisotopic residue masses (Da). L/I are isobaric, already a hint at why
# de novo is hard.
RESIDUE_MASS = {
    "G": 57.02146, "A": 71.03711, "S": 87.03203, "P": 97.05276,
    "V": 99.06841, "T": 101.04768, "C": 103.00919, "L": 113.08406,
    "I": 113.08406, "N": 114.04293, "D": 115.02694, "Q": 128.05858,
    "K": 128.09496, "E": 129.04259, "M": 131.04049, "H": 137.05891,
    "F": 147.06841, "R": 156.10111, "Y": 163.06333, "W": 186.07931,
}
PROTON = 1.007276
WATER = 18.010565

def fragment_ions(peptide):
    """Return singly-charged b- and y-ion m/z arrays for a peptide string."""
    m = [RESIDUE_MASS[aa] for aa in peptide]
    b = [sum(m[:i]) + PROTON for i in range(1, len(peptide))]          # b_i
    y = [sum(m[i:]) + WATER + PROTON for i in range(1, len(peptide))]  # y_(n-i)
    return np.array(b), np.array(y)

peptide = "PEPTIDE"
b_ions, y_ions = fragment_ions(peptide)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.2))
ax.vlines(b_ions, 0, 1, color="#0b5e54", lw=2, label="b ions")
ax.vlines(y_ions, 0, 1, color="#c0392b", lw=2, label="y ions")
for i, mz in enumerate(b_ions, 1):
    ax.text(mz, 1.02, f"b{i}", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#0b5e54")
for j, mz in enumerate(sorted(y_ions), 1):  # y1 is the smallest C-term fragment
    ax.text(mz, 1.10, f"y{j}", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#c0392b")
ax.set_xlabel("m/z"); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.25); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title(f"Theoretical spectrum: {peptide}")
ax.legend(loc="center right")
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("theoretical-spectrum.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Theoretical singly-charged b/y spectrum for PEPTIDE. The whole de novo problem is: recover the sequence given only these peaks (plus noise, missing peaks, and unknown charge).](index_files/figure-html/fig-theoretical-spectrum-output-1.png){#fig-theoretical-spectrum width=662 height=298}
:::
:::


> Note the consecutive mass gaps: `b2 − b1` is exactly the mass of residue 2.
> That single observation is the seed of every classical de novo algorithm, and
> the thing a neural model has to learn implicitly.

## Why this is hard (and where models earn their keep)

> A short list you can expand: missing peaks, noise peaks, isobaric residues
> (I/L), near-isobaric pairs (K/Q, GG/N), unknown precursor charge, PTMs,
> co-isolation. Each one breaks the clean picture above.

## From mass gaps to a model

> Bridge to the real work: the spectrum graph / dynamic-programming view, then
> why sequence-to-sequence models (InstaNovo, InstaNovo+) reframe it, and what
> confidence estimation (Winnow) adds on top. Link your own papers here.

## Try it yourself

> Suggested exercises for the reader: add a-ions and neutral losses; add a
> single PTM mass to one residue and watch the series shift; corrupt the
> spectrum with random peaks and see how mass-gap reading degrades.
